coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent folic acid-capped copper nanoclusters for the determination of rifampicin based on inner filter effect
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yi
Deng, Qingbo
Tang, Chang
Zhang, Minglu
Huang, Zilong
Cai, Zhifeng
coredata.dc:description: Development of excellent sensors to determine trace concentrations of rifampicin is of intense importance for medicine analysis and human health. Herein, a facile and green fluorescent probe was established for the determination of rifampicin by using folic acid protected copper nanoclusters (FA-Cu NCs). Many characterization methods were applied for the analysis of the as-prepared FA-Cu NCs including UV–visible absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), transmission electron microscope (TEM), fluorescence lifetime and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The TEM image suggested that the as-prepared FA-Cu NCs were highly dispersed. The as-synthesized FA-Cu NCs emerged blue fluorescence under UV light and demonstrated maximum emission wavelength at 446 nm under the maximum excitation wavelength of 358 nm. After the addition of rifampicin, the FL intensities of FA-Cu NCs were uncommonly quenched. The related experimental data intimated that the quenching mechanisms were assumed to the inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching. The as-proposed probe platform displayed an obvious linear relationship with rifampicin concentrations varying from 0.5 to 100 µM, and the corresponding detection limit (LOD) was 0.073 µM (S/N = 3). Finally, the as-established detection platform was successfully employed to analyze trace concentrations of rifampicin in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper nanoclusters
Folic acid
Fluorescence determination
Rifampicin

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing sensitivity of FA-Cu NCs to rifampicin
Determination of rifampicin in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Hubei University of Arts and Science

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Doctoral Startup Foundation of Hubei University of Arts and Science

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We would like to thank the Student Innovation Training Program (No.202110519017) for financial support, the Doctoral Startup Foundation of Hubei University of Arts and Science (No.2059135).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The UV–vis absorption spectra of FA, N2H4·H2O and CuCl2 and FA-Cu NCs; (B) The fluorescence spectra of FA, N2H4·H2O and CuCl2 and FA-Cu NCs; (C) The fluorescence emission spectra of FA-Cu NCs under different excitation wavelength (varying from 345 to 380 nm); (D) The maximum fluorescence excitation and emission wavelength of FA-Cu NCs.
(A) TEM image of FA-Cu NCs; (B) The XPS spectrum of Cu2p.

(A) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of rifampicin, the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of FA-Cu NCs; (B) The fluorescence lifetime of FA-Cu NCs and FA-Cu NCs/rifampicin system; (C) The UV–vis absorption spectra of FA-Cu NCs, rifampicin and FA-Cu NCs + rifampicin; (D) The linear relationship between F0/F value and the concentrations of rifampicin (0.5–30 μM).
(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of FA-Cu NCs with different concentrations of rifampicin; (B) The linear relationship between ln(F0/F) value and the concentrations of rifampicin (0.5–100 μM).
Relative fluorescence intensities (F0/F) of FA-Cu NCs solution with rifampicin and other competitive substances (The amounts of each references were 100 μM).
Schematic illustration of the preparation process of FA-Cu NCs.
Comparison of different analytical methods for rifampicin sensing.
The detection of rifampicin in real samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Fluorescent folic acid-capped copper nanoclusters for the determination of rifampicin based on inner filter effect

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Hubei University of Arts and Science, Xiangyang 441053, Hubei Province, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei University of Arts and Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical Engineering, Hubei University of Arts and Science, Xiangyang 441053, Hubei Province, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong 030619, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong 030619, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Development of excellent sensors to determine trace concentrations of rifampicin is of intense importance for medicine analysis and human health. Herein, a facile and green fluorescent probe was established for the determination of rifampicin by using folic acid protected copper nanoclusters (FA-Cu NCs). Many characterization methods were applied for the analysis of the as-prepared FA-Cu NCs including UV–visible absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), transmission electron microscope (TEM), fluorescence lifetime and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The TEM image suggested that the as-prepared FA-Cu NCs were highly dispersed. The as-synthesized FA-Cu NCs emerged blue fluorescence under UV light and demonstrated maximum emission wavelength at 446 nm under the maximum excitation wavelength of 358 nm. After the addition of rifampicin, the FL intensities of FA-Cu NCs were uncommonly quenched. The related experimental data intimated that the quenching mechanisms were assumed to the inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching. The as-proposed probe platform displayed an obvious linear relationship with rifampicin concentrations varying from 0.5 to 100 µM, and the corresponding detection limit (LOD) was 0.073 µM (S/N = 3). Finally, the as-established detection platform was successfully employed to analyze trace concentrations of rifampicin in real samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, metal nanoclusters (M NCs) have attracted more and more attention because of their charmingly physical, chemical, optical, and electrochemical properties [1–3]. Kinds of M NCs, such as Au NCs, Ag NCs, Pt NCs and Cu NCs, have been successfully prepared through different synthesis methods. Compared with Au NCs, Ag NCs and Pt NCs, the cost of Cu NCs is relatively low, which is attributed to the rich copper content [4,5]. Additionally, the Cu NCs reveal much more salient stability, lower toxicity and convenience than carbon dots, quantum dots and organic dyes [6]. Thus, the Cu NCs are becoming more and more important in research materials and the preparation of Cu NCs have made great progress [7,8].

To date, some methods, such as chemical reduction method [9], photochemical method [10], microwave ultrasound method [11], ligand-capped method [12], electrochemical synthesis method [13], template-assisted method [14] and size-focusing etching method [15,16], have been successfully developed to prepare the Cu NCs. With the fast developing synthesis technologies, different protective agents, such as egg white [17,18], amino acids [19–21], proteins [22–24], polyethyleneimine [25,26], polyvinylpyrrolidone [27], glutathione [28–30], lipoic acid [31,32] and ascorbic acid [33], have been applied to modify and stabilize the Cu NCs. At the same time, the widely used reducing agents mainly include hydrazine hydrate, sodium borohydride, ascorbic acid, hydroxylamine hydrochloride, sodium thiosulfate and so on [34–38]. Moreover, the Cu NCs have been widely used to detect metal ions, non-metal ions, small biological molecules and drugs [39–42].

Rifampicin (Fig. S1), an effective broad-spectrum antibiotic, has good antibacterial activity against Gram-positive cocci and Mycobacterium tuberculosis [43,44]. It has been applied for the treatment of Hansen’s disease (HD) and tuberculosis [45]. However, rifampicin also has more side effects, such as vomiting, upset stomach, liver damage, rash, itching, fever, hemolytic anemia, etc [46]. It is very meaningful to analyze the drug levels in medicine samples when its concentrations reached a toxic level. Thus, some methods for the determination of rifampicin in real samples have been successfully developed, including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [47,48], mass spectrometry [49], liquid chromatography/tandem mass spectrometry [50], chemiluminescence [51], capillary zone electrophoresis [52], UV–visible spectrophotometry [53], spectrophotometry [54], liquid chromatography coupled with ultra-violet (LC-UV) [55], high performance thin layer chromatography (HPTLC) [56], electrochemical method [57,58], fluorescence method [59] and Raman spectroscopy [60]. Among these, although the assays of LC-UV, HPLC, HPTLC and electrochemical methods are also sensitive, but they require expensive instruments, professional operators and complicated procedure for pretreating samples, and they are not suitable for initial rapidly detection. The fluorescence methods for the determination of rifampicin based on fluorescent sensors have drawn intensive attention due to their captivating advantages like high sensitivity, cost-efficient and simple pre-procedure. Up to now, some fluorescent probes, such as carbon quantum dots [61], semiconductor quantum dots [62] and silver nanoclusters [63], have been applied for the analysis of other drugs. As we all know, there are few reports about the Cu NCs for rifampicin detecting.

In this paper, we established a facile and green fluorescence probe based on the copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) for rifampicin detecting. The Cu NCs were synthesized through folic acid (FA) as the protective agent and hydrazine hydrate as the reducing agent (Scheme 1 ). Intriguingly, the fluorescence of the FA-Cu NCs could be quenched with the addition of rifampicin due to the inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching. The amounts of rifampicin could be analyzed with excellent selectivity and sensitivity. Furthermore, this platform based on FA-Cu NCs was firstly employed for the rifampicin sensing in real samples.

The fluorescence and UV–Vis absorption spectra of the as-prepared FA-Cu NCs were conducted on an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo Japan) with the Ex/Em slits set at 10.0/10.0 nm and a Shimadzu 2450 UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), respectively. The morphology and size of the FA-Cu NCs were analyzed by the FEI Tecnai G2 F20 (United States) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The FLS-1000 combined fluorescence lifetime and steadystate spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK) were applied for the determination of fluorescence lifetimes of FA-Cu NCs with and without rifampicin. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis results of FA-Cu NCs were carried out on ESCALAB 250XI (United States). Moreover, the Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) of FA-Cu NCs and FA were performed on a FTIR-8400S (Shimadzu Corporation, Kyoto, Japan).

All chemical reagents were used for subsequent experiments without further purification. Copric chloride dihydrate (CuCl2·2H2O, AR), folic acid (FA, ≥97%), hydrazine hydrate (N2H4·H2O, 80%), potassium chloride (KCl, 99.5%), sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.5%), silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99.8%), cadmium chloride (CdCl2, 99%), magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2·6H2O, 99.99%), manganese chloride tetrahydrate (MnCl2·4H2O, 99%), calcium chloride dehydrate (CaCl2·2H2O, AR), lead chloride (PbCl2, AR), zinc chloride (ZnCl2, 98%), aluminum chloride (AlCl3, 99%), cobalt chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2·6H2O, AR), sodium bromide (NaBr, 99%), sodium iodide (NaI, 99.5%), sodium nitrate (NaNO3, 99%), sodium carbonate monohydrate (Na2CO3·H2O, 99%), sodium sulfate anhydrous (Na2SO4, 99%), sodium phosphate tribasic dodecahydrate (Na3PO4·12H2O, ≥98%), phenylalanine (99%), lysine (97%), glycine (≥99%), alanine (99%), tyrosine (99%), cetirizine (98%), kanamycin sulfate (98%), busulfan (98%), dapsone (98%), chlorambucil (>98%) and rifampicin (97%) were obtained from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultra-pure water (18.25 MΩ·cm−1) was purified using a Millipore Milli-Q.

The folic acid-protected copper nanoclusters were established through a facile, green, rapid one-pot synthetic method [64]. Firstly, 0.1768 g of FA was dissolved into CuCl2 solution (6 mL, 0.05 M) and stirred for 5 min. Then, the N2H4·H2O (30 μL) was dropped into the above solution at 35 °C for 18 h. Finally, the product solution was centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 20 min and the clear solution was obtained for further use.

To detect rifampicin, a determination system was established based on the FA-Cu NCs (2.0 mL). A series of FA-Cu NCs with rifampicin were configured for the linear research (the different concentrations of rifampicin were 0, 0.5, 1, 3, 5, 7, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90 and 100 μM). Then, the analysis platform was placed at room temperature for 20 s. The fluorescence spectrometer was applied for the record of the fluorescence emission data at 446 nm.

To explore the selectivity of the FA-Cu NCs for rifampicin, the interfering experiments were performed under the same conditions (the control contained K+, Na+, Ag+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Co2+, Br−, I−, NO3 −, CO3 −, SO4 2−, PO4 3−, phenylalanine, lysine, glycine, alanine, tyrosine, cetirizine, kanamycin, busulfan, dapsone, chlorambucil). The amounts of each references were 100 μM.

To discuss the determination accuracy, rifampicin in bovine serum, milk and rifampicin capsules samples were performed. The bovine serum samples were obtained from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The milk samples were obtained from supermarket. Before the detection of rifampicin, the rifampicin capsules samples were dissolved in methanol. Then the obtainedsamples were filtered for the removal of larger suspended particles. Finally, the standard rifampicin solutions were added into the purified samples. The found concentrations of rifampicin were obtained through the linear equation (Section 3.3).

The optical performance, structure and composition of FA-Cu NCs were performed through fluorescence spectrophotometer, UV spectrophotometer, TEM, XPS and FT-IR, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 1 A, compared with FA, N2H4·H2O and CuCl2, a UV–vis absorption peak of FA-Cu NCs at 285 nm was observed. For FA-Cu NCs, no significant absorption peaks were found between 400 nm and 600 nm, indicating the absence of larger copper nanoparticles [65]. As exhibited in Fig. 1B, the fluorescence property difference between FA, N2H4·H2O and CuCl2 and FA-Cu NCs was investigated, showing that the fluorescence originated from FA-Cu NCs. To obtain optimal excitation and emission wavelength, the emission wavelength was discussed under different excitation wavelength. The emission wavelength remained basically constant, indicating the excitation-independent performance (Fig. 1C). Meanwhile, the optimal excitation and emission wavelength were 358 nm and 446 nm, respectively (Fig. 1D). The morphology and composition of FA-Cu NCs were further studied. The TEM image exhibited that the FA-Cu NCs were spherical and well-dispersed, and no large copper nanoparticles or aggregation emerged (Fig. 2 A). From the EDX analysis (Fig. S2), the elemental constituents of the FA-Cu NCs were confirmed: Cu is the core metal, and C, O, and N are present in the ligand structure, in agreement with the expected composition. Additionally, the XPS method was applied for the study of the elemental composition of FA-Cu NCs. The binding energy peaks at 287.09 eV, 401.78 eV, 533.56 eV, and 931.87 eV belonged to C1s, N1s, O1s and Cu2p of FA-Cu NCs, respectively (Fig. S3). As exhibited in Fig. 2B, two major peaks of Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2 clearly appeared in the copper XPS single spectrum of FA-Cu NCs, and no obvious peaks at 942 eV were obtained, demonstrating that the state of copper was Cu0 or Cu+ [66]. Moreover, the FT-IR spectrum was used to analyze the main functional groups of FA-Cu NCs. The feature peaks at 3417.11, 1270.84 and 1106.41 cm−1 belonged to N-H, C-N and C-O bond stretching vibration [64,67,68]. The peak at 1353.25 cm−1 was assumed to C-H bond deformation vibration [69]. The peaks at 1601.51, 1500.56 and 1423.08 cm−1 were ascribed to the skeletal vibration of the benzene ring [70]. (Fig. S4). The all characterization results exhibited that the FA-Cu NCs with blue fluorescence emission were successfully developed.

The stability of FA-Cu NCs at various storage time, UV irradiation time, NaCl concentrations and pH value were also discussed. As displayed in Fig. S5(A) and S5(B), the fluorescence intensities of FA-Cu NCs showed little change after 15 d of storage time and 2.5 min of UV irradiation time and remained extremely stable in high-ionicstrength solutions (c(NaCl) = 0.25 M) (Fig. S5(C)). As displayed in Fig. S5(D), with increasing pH value from 2 to 6, the fluorescence intensity significantly decreased. Except for pH value, the above experimental results showed that the FA-Cu NCs had excellent stability after long-term storage time and UV light irradiation time and in highionic-strength solutions.

The determination mechanism for rifampicin by FA-Cu NCs detection model was considered. The excitation and emission spectra of FA-Cu NCs and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of rifampicin were listed in Fig. 3 A. Obviously, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of rifampicin overlapped with the excitation and emission spectra of FA-Cu NCs to a large extent.. Then, the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or IFE might be the main quenching mechanism [71]. The fluorescence lifetime was further employed for the study of genre of quenching mechanism. The fluorescence lifetime of FA-Cu NCs (10.13 ns) did not change significantly with rifampicin (10.05 ns) (Fig. 3B), exhibiting that the static quenching was main mechanism and the dynamic quenching was excluded. Moreover, the change of fluorescence lifetime with and without quencher was the basis for the FRET mechanism. The lifetime results confirmed that the IFE was also the quenching mechanism [72]. The IFE resulted from the absorption of excitation or emission spectrum of the fluorescent probe by the quencher, which could cause the fluorescence to weaken or even disappear [73]. As we all know, the static quenching is due to the formation of non-luminescent ground-state complexes between the fluorescent molecule and the quencher [74]. To further verify the static quenching, the UV spectroscopy was used to study the interaction between FA-Cu NCs and rifampicin. As displayed in Fig. 3C, two new peaks of FA-Cu NCs + rifampicin spectra at 269.47 and 338.39 nm appeared, indicating that the fluorescence mechanism was the static quenching. Additionally, the static quenching also could be investigated through the Stern-Volmer equation (F0/F = 1 + K SV[Q] = 1 + K qτ0[Q]) [75,76]. K SV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. [Q] represents the amount of rifampicin, and τ0 is the lifetime of the FA-Cu NCs (10.13 ns). As shown in Fig. 3D, K SV and K q were calculated to be 5.1 × 104 L mol−1 and 5.03 × 1012 L mol−1 s−1, respectively. The K q value was greater than the maximum scatter collision quenching constant (2 × 1010 L·mol−1·s−1), indicating that the fluorescence quenching mechanism was attributed to static quenching. Then, the IFE and static quenching were the main mechanisms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing sensitivity of FA-Cu NCs to rifampicin

ce:section.ce:para: The influence of possible controls existed in real samples on the detection system for rifampicin were discussed. The parallel interfering tests of series of metal and non-metal ions and small biological molecules were performed under the same determination conditions, such as K+, Na+, Ag+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Co2+, Br−, I−, NO3 −, CO3 −, SO4 2−, PO4 3−, phenylalanine, lysine, glycine, alanine, tyrosine, cetirizine, kanamycin, busulfan, dapsone, chlorambucil. The relative fluorescence intensity F0/F of FA-Cu NCs in the presence of series of metal and non-metal ions and small biological molecules were exhibited in Fig. 5 . After the addition of series of metal and non-metal ions and small biological molecules, the F0/F value could not vary essentially. In short, this as-developed FA-Cu NCs sensor possessed excellent selectivity for rifampicin, which existed a certain application value for the selectivity sensing of rifampicin in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of rifampicin in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the possible meaning of FA-Cu NCs for rifampicin sensing in real samples, rifampicin accounts in bovine serum and milk samples were analyzed through spiked methods. The rifampicin accounts in bovine serum and milk samples were obtained by the linear fit equation in Fig. 4B (ln(F0/F) = 0.024[Q] + 0.1399). The test results were exhibited in Table 2 , the 91.7–108.1% of recovery rate were obtained, and the RSD was lower than 3.28%. The accounts of rifampicin detected by this fluorescent method were basically close to the spiked concentrations, suggesting that this developed FA-Cu NCs-based detection method was reliable strategy for the sensing of rifampicin.

In short, the blue emission FA-Cu NCs with excellent performances were developed successfully through reduction strategy (CuCl2 as the precursor, FA as the protector and N2H4·H2O as the reducing agent). The whole preparation process was facile and environmentally friendly (the required medicines were common and preparation conditions were mild). The as-developed FA-Cu NCs held blue fluorescence under UV light. Interestingly, the rifampicin could quench the fluorescence of FA-Cu NCs based on the IFE and static quenching mechanism. The as-synthesized FA-Cu NCs could be employed to analyze the rifampicin, and the linear range was 0.5–100 μM and the detection limit was 0.073 μM. Therefore, the as-prepared FA-Cu NCs possessed excellent sensitivity and selectivity, wider linear range and lower detection limit for the determination of rifampicin. At the same time, this detection platform was applied in real samples.

Yi Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Qingbo Deng: Measure fluorescence. Chang Tang: Measure fluorescence. Minglu Zhang: Measure fluorescence. Zilong Huang: Measure UV-visible spectra, Performing the experiments. Zhifeng Cai: Grammar correction, Sample characterization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank the Student Innovation Training Program (No.202110519017) for financial support, the Doctoral Startup Foundation of Hubei University of Arts and Science (No.2059135).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121944.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
